About Children and Children-No-Longer. Collected Papers 1942–1980

By Paula Heimann. Edited by Margret Tonnesmann. New Library of Psychoanalysis. London and New York: Tavistock/Routledge. 1989. Pp. ix + 368.

Gregorio Kohon

Gregorio Kohon

Gregorio Kohon
P. Box. 584, Spring Hill 4004, Australia

Gregorio Kohon  

Paula Heimann had been preparing her psychoanalytic papers for publication for many years when her task was interrupted by her death. She was then 83 years old, and had been interested and involved in psychoanalysis for 55 years. Margret Tonnesmann completed the task of editing this collection of 24 scientific papers with great care and dedication.

Paula Heimann was one of the most inspiring teachers and creative thinkers that the British Psycho-Analytical Society has ever had. Born in Danzig from Russian parents in 1899, she first trained as an analyst at the Berlin Institute, with Theodor Reik as her training analyst. Like other Jewish colleagues from the Continent, she moved to London in 1933, having been recommended by Max Eitingon to Ernest Jones. In the Introductory Memoir by Pearl King, included at the beginning of the book, we learn of the circumstances in which Paula Heimann arrived in England, how she became a patient of Melanie Klein, and subsequently one of her most loyal supporters. King describes how, from 1949 to her resignation from the Melanie Klein Trust in 1955, Paula Heimann gradually moved from being Melanie Klein's 'crown princess' (as she was considered by many) to becoming a true Independent analyst, a name that she favoured to the alternative 'Middle group'.

In her Introduction, the Editor summarizes some of the main theoretical and clinical developments of Paula Heimann's psychoanalytic contributions in the specific areas of early development; the role of aggression and destructive impulses; and the consequent changes in technique. For those who still doubt the complex, subtle, intricate interrelationship of theory and technique, this book is a wonderful illustration of it. Changes in Heimann's theoretical development are followed by clear technical changes.

For many years, Paula Heimann was better known for her paper 'On Counter-transference', published in 1950, and for her contributions to the 'Controversial Discussions', which clearly showed her commitment to Melanie Klein's thoughts. Her position as regards countertransference was bold, short, and to the point. Countertransference was understood as a concept that covered all the feelings (she speaks of 'the analyst's emotional response')

[[RunningHead]]

The British Psychological Society the William Inman Prize

Dr Samuel William Inman died on 26 August 1968 and in his will left the sum of £1000 to the Society:

… upon Trust that the Council shall invest the sum in such a manner as they shall think fit and with the income thereafter provide an annual prize for what the Society shall consider the best original work on psychosomatic ophthalmology in The British Journal of Medical Psychology or other medical journal approved by the Society.

In consideration of the legacy the said Council shall undertake to name the said prize as "The William Inman Prize".

In 1968 it was determined by the Council that the prize would be given for 'the best original work on psychosomatic ophthalmology published in The British Journal of Medical Psychology or other medical journal approved by the Society'. Normally the recipient would be a British subject, no person would receive the award on more than one occasion and that the award would be made annually on the basis of work published in the previous two calendar years, but no award would be made in the absence of work of sufficient merit.

Each year in the Annual Report, the Accounts have shown the manner in which the William Inman Prize fund is invested and the sums now accumulated in the fund, but there have been few occasions in recent years when there have been any claims on the prize, though efforts to publicize its availability have also atrophied.

The Scientific Affairs Board wishes to re-stimulate awareness of the existence of the prize. The Editor of The British Journal of Medical Psychology has been sensitized to the possibility of appropriate articles being submitted for the prize. The Board recommends that the definition of 'psychosomatic ophthalmology' be interpreted widely to include any research on psychosomatic factors in physical illness but especially those concerning the eye. Authors of published articles, their supporters and Journal editors are invited to submit to the Scientific Affairs Board, eight copies of any article published in 1991 or 1992 which might be considered for the prize. If articles of sufficient merit are submitted, the Board will appoint a committee to select a 1993 recipient of the prize which, in keeping with the original terms for the award agreed in 1968, will be a donation to be spent on books or apparatus up to the value of £200.

The Schema in Clinical Psychoanalysis

By Joseph W. Slap and Laura Slap-Shelton. London: The Analytic Press. 1991. Pp. 166.

Alan Krohn

Alan Krohn

Alan Krohn
2011 Geddes Ave, Ann Arbor, MI 48104

Alan Krohn  

This thought-provoking, clearly written book, proposes a 'schema' theory as an alternative to both the structural and topographic models. At the risk of oversimplifying this model, I will briefly summarize it. This theory, drawing on Piaget's theory of child development, views the difficulties of adult neurotic patients as essentially a misunderstanding of the present due to the continuation of childhood models of perceiving and cognizing reality. The adult neurotic patient is seen as operating on sequestered schemas, those schemas being described as 'freeze frames' of past views of people and childhood modes of understanding (and misunderstanding) them.

The unitary schema, termed ego, consists of the reality oriented, adaptive complex of ideas and the sequestered schema is organized 'around traumatic events and situations in childhood that were not mastered and assimilated …' When this sequestered schema leads to distress or symptoms it is termed a pathogenic schema. The sequestered schema, then, is a view of people, self, and the environment, that was formed at the traumatic point in development and contains views, cognitive style, and fantasies of that age period.

The goals the authors have set for themselves are important and worthy ones: to create a model that is close to clinical data, a model that integrates data from developmental psychology (here Piaget in particular), and a model that helps analysts make better interpretations to their patients. In some respects the authors achieve their goals while in more, in my opinion, they do not. Even in the areas where the goals are achieved, one has the gnawing feeling that existing theory and certainly existing practice by most competent analysts already comprehend these 'new' findings.

The notion that experiences of trauma are sequestered off and the mode of experience at the time of the trauma (the cognitive level of the child at the time) is also walled off, and that both lead to distortions later, is useful and true. While it is valuable to have this emphasized, is this really new and can this observation not very comfortably be integrated into our existing theory, especially the topographic?

[[RunningHead]]

The Isakower Phenomenon Revisited: A Case Study

O. Dann

O. Dann

O. Townsend Dann
4550 S.W. 74 Street
Miami
Florida 33143-6271

O. Dann  

SUMMARY

The Isakower phenomenon is a situation in which an individual typically experiences perceptions of enlargement or thickening, altered states of consciousness, sensations of floating, and impressions of the emergence and disappearance of an oral mass. This paper defines the phenomenon, reviews the literature, and describes a case in which a patient experienced it. The patient manifested the phenomenon symptomatically in the aftermath of a toxic state, and the elements were analysed for several years. There was support for Isakower's original thesis that the regressive states serve to defend against threatening incestuous fantasies. However, the Isakower phenomenon in this patient was the result of a multiplicity of determinants, largely defensive, involving many developmental levels.

Pietro Castelnuovo-Tedesco  

This, in every way, is a large, impressive work that will be seen by psychoanalysts everywhere as a fundamental intellectual contribution. It is, at the same time, a collection of seminal papers on a broad range of topics and a record of the intellectual development of a major contemporary figure in the field of psychoanalysis, Leo Rangell. This book, in two volumes, has exceptional reach and scope, both as to subject matter and as to the period it covers. It represents the intense, indeed passionate, investment in psychoanalysis of one who has been at the centre of psychoanalytic activity and discourse for more than forty years. Well known and recognized the world over (he was twice President of the American Psychoanalytic Association and twice President of the International Psychoanalytical Association), Rangell is deeply informed not only about psychoanalysis as a discipline, but also about psychoanalysts as individuals, in groups, and as an aggregate. A razor-sharp mind capable of cutting through all forms of confusion and illogic, extensive clinical experience, profound commitment to psychoanalysis and a special flair for psychoanalytic theory and metapsychology, are the major planks of the platform from which Rangell speaks. The perspective that he offers us from this platform is indeed exceptional. For these reasons, this is also a challenging work to review; it deals with clinical matters, with fundamental questions of theory, with complex organizational and political issues and, finally, with their evolution over time. It concerns the future of psychoanalysis. All this is there.

Titled The Human Core. The Intrapsychic Base of Human Behavior, this work stakes out at once its ambitious goal, namely its focus on intrapsychic events as they reach out to, and are influenced by, the multiplicity of human experience. Throughout, Rangell is cognizant of this macrocosm and strives to keep it in balance. He gives major emphasis to the ego's exercise of unconscious choice and carefully describes the many intermediate steps between impulse and action (or other psychic outcomes) which constitute the decision-making process. He calls 'this center of psychic activity "the human core"' (Vol I, p. 13). The papers in these two volumes were selected from a much larger bibliography because they deal, at various levels, with these central issues.

Fred Volkmar  

The ambitious nature of the present volume is suggested by its title. Dr Greenspan provides a model of ego development informed both by clinical observations and by research findings from the perspectives of psychoanalysis, psychiatry and developmental psychology. He outlines a model of ego development, broadly defined, in which the active organization of experience is present from very early in life and in which characteristic organizational levels and adaptive and maladaptive organizations can be identified. This model of ego development owes a particular debt to object-relations theory as well as to basic child development research. In this developmental structuralist view, normative ego development is seen as progressing through characteristic, successive phases.

During the phase of homeostasis (birth to three months) the infant must simultaneously take an interest in the world and begin to self-regulate.

On the Shoulders of Freud

By Roberto Speziale-Bagliacca, translated by D. E. W. Jones. New Brunswick and London: Transaction. 1991. Pp. 98.

Paola Mariotti

Paola Mariotti

Paola Mariotti
37 Woodsome Rd, London NW5 1SA

Paola Mariotti  

In his book the Italian psychoanalyst Dr Speziale-Bagliacca sets out to investigate some of the factors which may hamper the development and growth of psychoanalytic thought. He discusses some of Freud's writings and correspondence, from the point of view of the influence he had on his followers' theories and on the establishment of psychoanalytic institutions; he also explores, and by implication contrasts, Lacan's theoretical framework and his relation towards his own disciples.

What is being questioned seems to be the character of psychoanalytic knowledge, and the way this can be warped or fostered in the practice of psychoanalysis. In this respect the internal figures present in the psychoanalyst's inner world are of course crucial, and Freud, as the founder father of psychoanalysis, is a figure all psychoanalysts carry within, an all powerful ideal which some attack endlessly, and some revere blindly. And yet, Speziale-Bagliacca suggests, if one is to be able to express freely his or her creativity, one has to come to terms with such idealization, to which Freud himself contributed.

According to the author, a turning point in psychoanalysis was represented by Melanie Klein, who put the figure of the mother in the centre of the individual's inner world, shifting the emphasis from a phallocentric theory of development to an earlier and more complex relationship and opening the way to the concept of containment which was later developed by Bion. If I understand Speziale-Bagliacca's thought, implicit in the idea of containment is a suggestion of reciprocity, which he describes, in the psychoanalytic setting, as the circularity of transference: what he seems to mean by this term is the influence that the analyst's interpretations have on the patient, while the patient by 'fitting in' with the analyst's framework, confirms the theories underlying the interpretations.

It follows that the more rigid the analyst's approach is, the narrower are the limits in which the patient's communications will be contained or understood and any variation accepted. As a consequence the patient who becomes an analyst will be inclined to reproduce the same narrow structure, not only with his or her patients but also in the institutions in which the work takes place.

By Simon Grolnick. Northvale, New Jersey and London: Jason Aronson. 1990. Pp. 222.

F. Rodman

F. Rodman

F. Robert Rodman
450 North Bedford Drive #211 Beverly Hills, California 90210

F. Rodman  

In 1951, Winnicott's essay on transitional objects established his place as a completely new kind of psychoanalytic thinker. Collections of papers appeared in 1958 and 1965, and when he died in January of 1971, he was working on the page proofs of two of his greatest works, Playing and Reality and Therapeutic Consultations in Child Psychiatry. Numerous posthumous books have since been added to his bibliography and as we approach the centenary of his birth in 1996, worldwide interest in his work is enormous.

His contributions are so idiosyncratic and so diverse that a sense of the whole has been inaccessible short of a careful study of the total output. Boundary and Space(1983), co-authored by the leading authority on Winnicott, Madeleine Davis, who died in 1991, was the first attempt to summarize his work; Winnicott and Paradox(1987), by Clancier & Kalmanovitch, was another, less extensive, but valuable effort; and a book by Adam Phillips, called simply Winnicott(1988) is an eloquent statement of the essentials.

In The Work and Play of Winnicott, Dr Grolnick attempts to provide us with a summary and critique of Winnicott's work.

SUMMARY

Problems relating to the treatment of deployed patients are described and discussed through the case material on David Fisher. Since the deployments are not merely the defences or resistances we meet in neurotics, but a way of life with which these persons are strongly identified—deploying forces against the subjective experience of psychic pain—a different approach is felt to be required. I call this an affect-focused approach. Some of the major curative factors which have been found useful in persons with deployment are described and illustrated.

L. Guttieres-Green  

SUMMARY

The development of this clinical case, that of a young mother who was a painter, illustrates the link between motherhood and sublimated creativity. The importance of male identifications, the difficulties of identification with the mother and the guilt connected with penis envy are emphasized. Castration anxiety is attached to the child, experienced as a penis equivalent. Anal fixation also plays a crucial part, aggravating the refusal of an excessively guilt-ridden genital sexuality. The vicissitudes and conditions of female sublimation are discussed.

[[RunningHead]]

The Freud–Klein Controversies 1941–45

Edited by Pearl King and Riccardo Steiner. London and New York: New Library of Psychoanalysis. Tavistock/Routledge. 1991. Pp. xxvii + 958.

William Gillespie

William Gillespie

William Gillespie
4 Eton Villas, London NW3 4SX

William Gillespie  

The compilation and editing of this large book is a massive achievement; Pearl King and Riccardo Steiner have earned the gratitude not only of those working in the field of psychoanalysis but also of those in the wider field of scientific research. The book presents us with a very detailed and unexpurgated account of the various controversies in the British Psycho-Analytical Society that came to a head during the Second World War. It may also throw light on the more general matter of controversies in various spheres of learning, how they are sometimes contaminated by more personal factors, and perhaps how they may be in some measure resolved. This wider relevance of the psychoanalytic discussions is brought out by Pearl King in her Introduction, and in this connexion she refers to the very valuable 1985 paper of her co-editor, Riccardo Steiner. Her brief introduction is preceded by a useful series of biographical notes on 28 of the participants in the discussions.

Pearl King follows her introduction with a valuable and fair description of the historical background leading up to the Freud–Klein controversies. The central role of Ernest Jones, not merely as founder of the British Society, but as its undisputed leader for 25 years is described clearly. As Edward Glover once said in my hearing: 'The trouble about Jones is that he does everything so much better than anyone else'. Apart from his promotion of the science of psychoanalysis Jones was also the man, as King makes clear, mainly responsible for the recognition of its scientific status by the British medical profession and its exclusive right to the designation 'psychoanalysis'.

In the first years after the British Society was founded in 1919 there was no organized training but it was recognized that a personal analysis was necessary for a would-be analytic practitioner. In order to obtain this most of the early British analysts went to Vienna, Berlin or Budapest. In 1925 the Stracheys (James and Alix, not Jones and Alix Strachey, as wrongly printed on p. 18), working in Berlin, became fascinated by Melanie Klein, and this led to an invitation for her to give a series of lectures in London. Following this Jones invited her to settle in London. She accepted this the more readily in 1926 because her principal supporter in Berlin, Karl Abraham, had met an early death at the end of 1925.

[[RunningHead]]

The Prisonhouse of Psychoanalysis

By Arnold Goldberg. Hillsdale, N.J.: Analytic Press. 1990. Pp. 175.

Alan Eisnitz

Alan Eisnitz

Alan J. Eisnitz
275 Central Park West, Suite 1A, New York, NY 10024

Alan Eisnitz  

Arnold Goldberg contends that we, psychoanalysts and psychoanalysis, have imprisoned ourselves. As a result, our freedom of intellect, our creativity, the development of our field, the willingness and ability of psychoanalysis to take its place in the world of science, interacting with, evolving from, and contributing to other sciences, are all restricted.

As a secondary, corollary theme, he maintains that analytic concepts which are not deemed to be 'authentic', principally self psychology and by implication any set of ideas which are too challenging, are also imprisoned in the sense that they are subject to disapproval and criticism and are not given full access to acceptance and the encouragement to develop. Here, the 'jailers' are the leaders of more traditional psychoanalysis and its standards, including the American Psychoanalytic Association, and, at least in the US, the psychoanalytic journals.

Goldberg notes that the psychoanalytic arena of activity, the clinical situation, is subject to multiple perspectives and conclusions, and places many conflicting and stressful demands upon the analyst. This contributes to the tendency to seek comfort and security by means of a process similar to addiction. Just as the addict attempts to fill a structural defect with an idealized substance, the analyst (and the field of psychoanalysis) can idealize psychoanalytic heroes or concepts and become addicted to them out of a need to maintain a comforting tie. This tie must not be broken; the idealization must be continued and promoted by adherence to the tenets of the hero or theory. Freud is offered as the principal example of such a psychoanalytic hero as well as Kohut, Lacan and others.

It is not only the tie to the hero, understood here mainly along the lines of a self-object relationship, which is regarded as restricting the analyst's capacity for independent development, but also the addiction to theory which can be even more restrictive. For the addictive concept must be preserved, protected and promoted. Conformity is required, both for the self and for others. One's own ideas must follow the accepted idealized line, and the ideas of others are evaluated according to the degree to which they follow the prescribed rules. If they do not, they must be regarded as threatening.

The Goals of Psychoanalysis. Identification, Identity and Supervision.

By León Grinberg. London and New York: Karnac Books. 1990. Pp. 404.

Irene Matthis

Irene Matthis

Irene Matthis
St Eriksgatan 25, 112 39 Stockholm

Irene Matthis  

The Goals of Psychoanalysis is a collection of articles, in four parts. The first and last are extensive investigations of specific and circumscribed fields: 'The theory of identification' and 'Theoretical and clinical aspects of supervision'. In my reading they dialogue with each other, but they could just as well have been published as separate books. They would have earned their place in the working library of any analyst. As a matter of fact the hundred-paged section on identification, with all its sub-groupings and ramifications, could be used as a book of reference by any student of the theories and techniques dealing with processes of internalization and externalization.

The second, shortest, part of the book also deals with a special theme: 'The sense of identity', while the third part presents a collage of articles on many different psychoanalytical subjects, theoretical as well as clinical.

The author, León Grinberg, initially trained and worked as a psychoanalyst in Argentina. As the political situation in the country made psychoanalytical practice more and more difficult, he, in 1976, accepted an invitation to come to Madrid. Franco had recently died and the process of democratization in Spain had just started. At the time there was only a handful of analysts in Madrid. Today there are 45 and, with about 100 candidates in training, it has become a big society. León Grinberg has played an important role in this development.

This means that Grinberg's experience of psychoanalysis and of discussions and evolutions within psychoanalytical institutions covers a great part of the Spanish-speaking world. For a long time Spanish-speaking analysts have also been able to read him. The aim of this book is therefore to present to English-speaking colleagues, Grinberg's thoughts about psychoanalysis, its theories and techniques. To have this, so to speak, 'Spanish' perspective presented (of course it is only one of many possible perspectives) by way of an extensive collection of articles from one of its leading figures is interesting, especially for those who, because of language difficulties and/or geographical distances, know very little of what goes on south of the English-speaking psychoanalytical world.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychotic Anxieties and Containment. A Personal Record of an Analysis with Winnicott

By Margaret I. Little. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson. 1990. Pp. 129.

Olivier Flournoy

Olivier Flournoy

Olivier Flournoy
45 avenue de Champel, 1206 Geneva, Switzerland

Olivier Flournoy  

Donald W. Winnicott's admirers, whether psychoanalysts or not, will be delighted with this book. Here is the great man, in full action, handling, holding, curing, healing a well-known psychoanalyst who unveils memories of her youth, her family, her sickness for the benefit of the reader.

Margaret Little holds us spellbound from the first to the last paragraph of her book, so rich and condensed in spite of its apparent brevity.

Written with clarity and alertness, it captivates the reader as a good novel or even a thriller does. At other times it leads us to the heart of difficult theoretical and practical problems such as regression. Nevertheless, in my opinion, these problems are tackled in such a way that they will not discourage the non-analyst reader, holding his interest as well as that of the analyst.

One's interest is held by these 120 odd pages, filled with emotion, enthusiasm and gratitude from a patient who claims to have been a psychotic borderline case cured by a treatment (a psychoanalysis?) conducted like a fully-fledged battle against death, by the couple Winnicott-Little, a kind of living metaphor of a mother and her toddler.

It is of course when the reading is done that all sorts of questions arise, as if one had to wake up, open one's eyes and place oneself at a suitable distance in order to regain some critical sense. About the problem of reality, for instance, the writer's talent makes us believe in the factual reality of Margaret Little's history—her childhood, the circumstances of her life—a reality which makes us forget that it is constructed and interpreted through the double process of representation and writing. And Winnicott's comments strengthen one's feeling of uneasiness, when, for example, he cries out his hatred of this woman—the patient's mother—such a monster … (who, by the way, was still alive at the time). There Winnicott seems to go beyond transference into identification and recognition of a reality mistaken for the veracity of his patient's tale.

The Protective Shell in Children and Adults

By Frances Tustin. London: Karnac Books. 1990. Pp. 241.

V. Hamilton

V. Hamilton

V. Hamilton
990 Hanky Avenue, Los Angeles, California 90049

V. Hamilton  

Tustin's evocative conceptualizations of autistic states escort us to the outer reaches, and thereby to the foundations, of human experience. Far reaches, because she asks us to imagine what it is like to live in a world in which the ordinary foundations and signposts have gone missing. We would indeed feel 'up in the air', 'unrooted', 'spinning endlessly', 'floating weightlessly'; to feel human again, we must 'come down to earth'. But the return journey requires the attention of a skilled, imaginative and steadfast escort since we must encounter the same primeval terrors, dread and sickness of heart from which we fled upwards and outwards in the first place. As one recovering autistic child remarked, 'It's such a worry being a human being'. Tustin states grimly, 'In these children the human spirit has been well-nigh extinguished'. All the mundane steps of daily life are worrisome for the autistic individual because the dimensions of space and time which underpin our commonsense world are not, and have never become, 'second nature'. They have to be learned or found as the unordinary, solipsistic, self-generated—autistic—protections drop off like a dessicated skin or brittle shell.

Kant might have felt vindicated by Tustin's phenomenological account of what he described as the 'a priori principles of knowledge'—namely 'space and time'. Kant said 'although all our knowledge begins with experience, it does not follow that it all arises out of experience'. There are absolute universal modes of knowledge—the pure intuitions of space and time—which we do not derive immediately from our own experience but from a universal rule which is 'borrowed by us from experience'. Thus, Kant says, 'we would say of a man who undermined the foundations of his house, that he might have known a priori that it would fall, that is, that he need not have waited for the experience of its actual falling. But still he could not know this completely a priori. For he had first to learn through experience that bodies are heavy, and therefore fall when their supports are withdrawn'. Space and time 'exist in the mind a priori as a mere form of sensibility … even without any actual object of the senses or of sensation. We can never represent to ourselves the absence of space, though we can quite well think it as empty of objects'. Frances Tustin's autistic children seem to have exerted superhuman willpower to erect a senseless asylum in which space and time—the foundations of all sensibility and thought—are ignored. Here they have foundered. For, in Kant's words, 'Without sensibility no object would be given to us, without understanding no object would be thought. Thoughts without content are empty, intuitions without concepts are blind'.

Marc, or the Soldier's Journey. The Elaboration of Passivity as a Factor of Change

Alain Gibeault

Alain Gibeault

Alain Gibeault
17 rue Albert Bayet
75013 Paris

Alain Gibeault  

SUMMARY

Change during analysis is here understood as the possibility of elaborating the 'biological bedrock' described by Freud as the repudiation of femininity in both sexes. The analysis of a man with anxiety hysteria permits us to show how temporal regression induced by the analytic setting enables the elaboration of passivity in its fantasy and object-related aspects. The return of this patient seven years after the end of the analysis in a moment of strange coincidence for the analyst, gave rise to a consideration of the difficulties and the issues in topographical and formal regression during analysis: the necessity of working on the dynamics of the narcissistic double and on the anxieties relating to the danger of non-representation and the loss of the capacity for investment. The working through of this primordial passivity, corresponding to active work, concerns analyst as well as analysand in their common work and constitutes the final point which all analyses should reach to achieve a durable change in its topographical, dynamic and economic aspects.

[[RunningHead]]

A Moment of Transition: Two Neuroscientific Articles by Sigmund Freud

Translated and edited by Mark Solms and Michael Saling. London: Institute of Psychoanalysis. London and New York: Karnac Books. 1990. Pp. 164.

Brian Martindale

Brian Martindale

Brian Martindale
31 Nassau Rd, Barnes, London SW13 9QF

Brian Martindale  

In spite of the substantial literature concerning the relevance of Freud's neurological background to the foundations of psychoanalysis, only seven of Freud's hundred neurological articles have previously been translated into English.

In this most important book, two further articles, 'Aphasie' and 'Gehirn' (Brain), are translated into English for the first time. Solms & Saling are both the translators and the editors. They provide a most scholarly commentary on issues of considerable contemporary relevance concerning the scientific origins and foundations of psychoanalytic theory.

The two articles translated and studied in this volume were written for a dictionary (Valleret's) in 1888. The authors give clear evidence that these articles were indeed written by Freud (the entries in the encyclopedia were not signed). By 1888, Freud had already visited Charcot, was using hypnosis and was writing and lecturing on hysteria. These articles were written therefore before Freud's famous articles 'On aphasia' (1891) and the 'Project' (1895) and as suggested in the title of the book occur at 'a moment of transition' when Freud was shifting his preoccupations from matters neurological to matters psychological.

Much of the academic criticisms of Freuds' early psychoanalytic ideas derives from judgements that these concepts are based on the accepted neurophysiological and neuroanatomical theories of his time. The fact that these latter postulates have been shown to be false has led to the argument that the early psychoanalytic concepts and foundations of metapsychology must also be false.

In a previous contribution, the authors of A Moment of Transition reviewed aspects of Freud's 'Project' and the discussions and controversies that have used that posthumous publication to debate the neurological foundation of Freud's psychoanalytic concepts (Solms & Saling, 1986). They provided convincing evidence that the major constructs used in the 'Project' were not based on the contemporary neurological or neurophysiological models of Freud's time, apart from the recently discovered neurons. They were in fact psychological hypotheses probably based on Freud's clinical studies.

La Babele Dell' Inconscio. Lingua Madre E Lingue Straniere Nella Dimensione Analitica. (The Babel of the Unconscious. Mother Tongue and Foreign Tongues in the Analytic Dimension.)

By J. Amati Mehler, S. Argentieri and J. Canestri. Milano: Raffaele Cortina Editore. 1990.

Adam Limentani

Adam Limentani

Adam Limentani
38 Penshurst Gardens, Middlesex HA8 9TP

Adam Limentani  

To write a review of a book written in a language that is not available to the majority of readers is fraught with some considerable risks as it might be tempting to say too much to the point of confusing them. The alternative is to condense the many intricate problems tersely described in the subtitle. This is what the authors attempted to do in the paper 'The babel of the unconscious' which appeared in the International Review of Psycho-Analysis in 1990, when they hardly did justice to their fascinating and disturbing book.

Language problems have always been with us, as revealed by myth, but psychoanalysts have been particularly exposed to them in so far as few of them have been able to read Freud's writings in German. The barrier of language which prevents many psychoanalysts from becoming acquainted with advanced research carried out in Latin America, the Scandanavian countries, Italy, Germany, etc. is reflected in the absence of appropriate references. This contribution by the authors has caused me to reflect on the fact that we are seldom informed about the language used in an analysis, even when it is apparent that one member of the dyad or even both of them are not using their mother tongue. (To my surprise I have recently come across a paper where it is described how a patient stipulated that the analysis should be carried out in an unusual language. When the analyst acceded to the request, there was harsh criticism from many colleagues.)

I do not believe I will be the only reader who will be subjected to a rush of memories and reminiscences when reading this volume. Having been compelled by external circumstances to have an analysis in English, it was not long before I became aware of the frustration arising from not being able ever to express my feelings in Italian. The experience was not easily forgotten. When in later years I tried to raise the issue, especially when I was becoming involved in educational matters, I was confronted by a blank wall, apart from some scepticism supported by the statement that 'it was the language of the unconscious that was important and that was the same for everyone'. This conclusion will be very reassuring to those who would like to believe that language does not matter in psychoanalytic therapy. It is, however, comforting to see that the authors do not lend their support to this statement in their final statement, although they express the view that the problem is not insurmountable.

Psychic Change in Child Analysis

Elizabeth Rocha Barros []

Elizabeth Rocha Barros

Elizabeth Lima Da Rocha Barros
Rua Bahia 71, Apto. 7A
01244-São Paulo-SP
Brazil

Elizabeth Rocha Barros  

SUMMARY

The paper describes a period of analysis of a 5-year-old boy as an illustration of some ideas about psychic change. Psychic change is approached in terms of emotional growth, which is directly associated with the degree of flexibility of the defensive system and its capacity to absorb and re-integrate lost parts of the ego and to be open to new emotional experiences.

It is described how, through interpretations, the child's internal objects and relationships changed. In particular, his representation of his bad-analyst, bad-father, bad-self changed from being a gorilla to the Incredible Hulk to the whale and finally to Superman crying.

When the analysis was interrupted, the patient was showing clear indications that the basis had been laid for establishing a more stable internalized object capable of containing and transforming his anxieties and feelings of despair into more tolerable ones, which gave him a better chance of being fed by good experiences.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Robert Stoller (1925–1991)

Estelle Shane

Estelle Shane

Morton
Estelle Shane
Suite 203, 1800 Fairburn Ave
Los Angeles, CA90025

Estelle Shane  

Robert J. Stoller died on 6 September 1991, at the age of 66, when the car in which he and his wife, Sybil, were driving was smashed by another vehicle speeding down Sunset Boulevard. While Bob suffered fatal injuries, mercifully Sybil, sitting next to him, was essentially unharmed. Bob was a man who lived by ideas, ideals and convictions, a person of unusual integrity, a man who was all of a piece. His friends are left only with the memory of a man who was admirable just by virtue of this very consistency, along with an existential acceptance of life's vicissitudes and its inexorable tragedy.

Robert Stoller was born and raised in Bronxville, New York. In 1941 he began his undergraduate education at Columbia University, interrupting his matriculation there after one year to volunteer for the service. Shunted from active duty to premedical studies at Stanford University, Bob completed his medical school at U.C. San Francisco, and after a psychiatric residency, he re-enlisted in the Air Force, serving as head of psychiatry at Travis Air Force Base from 1951–1953.

Bob remained profoundly affected, however, by World War II, in which many of his friends and classmates had perished. Consistent with his proclivity and capacity for intellectual mastery of what deeply troubled and preoccupied him, he became an expert in all matters concerning that war. He remained throughout his life passionately concerned with issues of world peace in particular, and humanitarian affairs in general, giving of himself and of his financial resources in support what he believed.

His most passionate concern, however, apart from his family, was his lifelong involvement with psychoanalysis. Beginning his academic career in 1954 with a faculty appointment in psychiatry at UCLA, he remained at UCLA, rapidly rising to the rank of full professor. Anyone who, during that period, attended medical school at the University, or served as a resident there, has learned psychiatry in one way or another from him, and everyone to whom he acted as teacher or mentor remembers, the experience and speaks of it now, especially, with great gratitude. Research analysts, in particular, were drawn to him, not only because he had shared their fascination with research per se, but also because Bob had such a wide range of interests, including history, anthropology, literature, sociology, and the field of academic medicine.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Autistic-Contiguous Position

R. Roche

R. Roche

R. Cruz Roche
Infanta M. Teresa 18
28016 Madrid

R. Roche  

Dear Sir,

I read with great interest the article by Dr T. H. Ogden: 'On the concept of an autisticcontiguous position' (Int. J. Psychoanal., 70: 127–140). His observations, which are of great clinical and theoretical value, hold no surprises for psychoanalysts whose language and culture are Spanish.

As long ago as 1962 in his article 'Modalities of the object relation [Modalidades de la relación objetal]' (Revista de Psicoanálisis, XIX: 1–2), and later in his book Symbiosis and Ambiguity [ Symbiosis y Ambigüedad ] (Buenos Aires: Paidós, 1967), Dr José Bleger of Buenos Aires proposed the concept of a 'glischro-caric1 position' (sic) as a psychological 'position' prior to the paranoid-schizoid and depressive positions described by M. Klein. The glischro-caric position, in which the characteristic type of anxiety would be confusional, would take as its object what Bleger called an 'ambiguous' or 'agglutinated nucleus' characterized by a lack of independence of the object, and its specific defences would be splitting, immobilization and fragmentation; and all this at a moment in development which is dominated by the most primitive feelings, particularly kinaesthetic sensations. These concepts of José Bleger's are grounded in numerous extended clinical examples.

Nearly 30 years later Dr T. H. Ogden describes, sometimes in very similar terms, concepts which are practically identical. It seems to me right that someone should reassert publicly in the Int. J. Psychoanal. the priority of the creative work of Dr Bleger, whose magnificent, rich and suggestive writings have not become as widely known as they deserve to be, perhaps because they have been written in Spanish.

Also, it seems right to recall, along with Dr Bleger, the productivity and orginality of the Argentinian school of psychoanalysis, which unfortunately is not very well known in European and North American circles, as I have had many occasions to point out.

This failure of communication gives rise to a duplication of effort in having to rethink and reformulate concepts which have already been formulated, wasting creative energies which could otherwise be applied to extending and deepening of our understanding.

[[RunningHead]]

The Wish for a Sex Change

Scott Carder M.D.

Scott Carder

Scott L. Carder, M.D.
2400 Mission Street
San Marino
California 91108

Scott Carder  

Dear Mr. Tuckett,

It was very exciting to see an article on transsexualism in the International Journal because this topic goes to the heart of some of our assumptions in psychoanalytic theory of identity, self, and change. There has not been a lot of reports about these types of sexual problems in our literature nor any reports of patients undergoing full psychoanalytic treatment before beginning hormone use or other physical treatments. The difficulty of getting these people into treatment is well discussed by Agnes Oppenheimer in her article, 'The Wish for a Sex Change: A Challenge to Psychoanalysis?' 1991, 72: 221–231.

Unfortunately neither of the two case reports in her paper were documented to be in full psychoanalytic treatment. The frequency of sessions, length of treatment, and the process of analysis (which includes the evolution and use of the transference relationship) was not stated in the first case and the second case is reported as coming once a week. From the data that is reported the author inferred that the patients defensively denied their 'real' sexual identity and were not open to interpretive efforts to address this resistance. She quickly dismisses Robert Stoller's long term research and conclusions on transsexualism as merely complying with the patient's wishes and therefore not helpful to analytic treatment of them. Oppenheimer states that she found an empathic attunement most helpful in getting analytic data from these patients but her interpretations and theoretical formulations indicated that she understood the problem only in an instinctual drive and defence model.

My impression from the article is that our theories still prevent us from deeper understanding of what our patients are experiencing. Why did Oppenheimer not have these people in full psychoanalytic therapy at four or five times per week where a deep experience of their transference relationship could occur? Only in this way will we be able to use our psychoanalytic tool of reliving the childhood in the transference as a means of really understanding these gender identity problems.

[[RunningHead]]

John D. Sutherland (1905–1991)

Paul O'Farrell

Paul O'Farrell

Paul O'Farrell
Royal Edinburgh Hospital
40 Colinton Road
Edinburgh EH10 5BT

Paul O'Farrell  

On 14 June 1991 Edinburgh lost its most active and distinguished psychoanalyst, just as 23 years earlier London had been bereft. Dr Jock Sutherland died at the age of 86. He had, among other things, been Editor of the International Psychoanalytical Library, k Editor of the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis, Chairman of the Tavistock Clinic and Trustee of the Menninger Foundation. What now need to be recorded are the experiences which allowed him to exert a wide influence upon colleagues over half a century.

He was born in Edinburgh on 23 April 1905, the son of a publisher. He was educated at George Heriot's School. From the age of 8 he wished to become a Medical Missionary. When he first went to Edinburgh University, it was to graduate in Chemistry in 1926. But then his interest in psychology asserted itself: he took a degree in both education and psychology and then was appointed lecturer in the Psychology Department. He was also a psychologist in the Edinburgh University Child Guidance Clinic. Whilst supervising psychology practicals, once the class had got down to work, he and his co-demonstrator, Dr Ronald Fairbairn, had opportunities to discuss psychoanalysis. Drawn to the kind of work Fairbairn did and the way he presented it, Sutherland decided to become a psychoanalyst. To this end Fairbairn encouraged him to take a medical degree and so he started his life-long habit of involving himself in three or more full-time occupations simultaneously. He took his PhD (the Speed Factor in Intelligent Reaction) in 1931 whilst working as a full-time Lecturer in Psychology and also as a Medical Student. He qualified MB ChB in 1937 and was Resident Medical Officer at the Glasgow Royal Mental Hospital and Lecturer in Psychological Medicine at Glasgow from 1938 to 1940.

He served in the Royal Army Medical Corps as a Major and was attached to Carstairs Hospital, Lanarkshire, which was a centre for the treatment of Army psychiatric casualties. He published a survey of a hundred cases of war neurosis in 1941 and in doing so contributed his first psychoanalytical observations and the most original made on battle neurotics since those of W. H. R. Rivers during the First World War. He identified, in a significant number of cases, an unconscious dread that the assertion of their independence was a dangerous aggressive process which would incur talionic parental vengeance.

What is Effective in Psychoanalytic Therapy: The Move from Interpretation to Relation

By W. W. Meissner. Northvale, NJ, and London: Jason Aronson. 1991. Pp. 217.

Stephen Post

Stephen Post

Stephen L. Post
4524 Forest Park Blvd, St Louis, MO 63108

Stephen Post  

The elegance of this volume lies in its combination of comprehensiveness and succinctness: with remarkably few words and in clear and simple terms the author reviews and appraises the evolution of psychoanalytic theories of therapy and their notions of what is, in James Strachey's language, mutative—or, to use Meissner's larger concept, effective.

To begin with, we are given Strachey's 1934 masterpiece, 'The nature of the therapeutic action of psychoanalysis', in its entirety. Inasmuch as Strachey was the first to identify interpretation as the specific mutative agency in analytic therapy, inclusion of his article gives us the definitive statement of that viewpoint; but it is equally felicitous as a way of summarizing the progress of theories of therapy up until Strachey's time.

Meissner then alludes to theories which have developed subsequently—ego psychology, object relations theory, self psychology, and developmental theory in particular, each with its own perspectives and contributions. Taken together, he suggests, they have moved us away from a narrow preoccupation with interpretation and 'toward a probing of those background conditions that are necessary for the therapeutic process to bring about effective change … Even if interpretation is the process by which understanding is achieved and insight generated, that process takes place within a relational matrix that both colors the interpretive process and provides those elements that make the process viable and meaningful' (p. 59).

The surprising thing [the author observes] 'is that, given all these contextual shifts in our understanding of the psychoanalytic process, Strachey's contribution remains to a large extent valid. [Subsequent developments] have not in the least undermined the validity of his observations about the role of interpretations and the significant part they play in the analytic process (p. 60).

Meissner is much less the revolutionary theoretical partisan than an evolutionary accommodationist, holding that the psychoanalytic point of view at its inception amounted to a paradigm shift but that its subsequent developments have not. It is in this spirit that he re-appraises Strachey's piece: Strachey's concept of the co-operative ego prefigures the therapeutic alliance; this latterday concept, if available to Strachey, could have been helpfully distinguished from the positive transference as an aid to overcome resistance; and again, the transformation of projective elements into more benign introjective components of the patient's auxiliary superego (Strachey's term for what is internalized from the analyst; Meissner prefers to view the internalization as not just engendering an auxiliary superego but entering the patient's self system as a whole) depends not on the positive transference, but on the alliance.

SUMMARY

The author presents clinical material from a 36-year-old female patient with depressive and hysterical traits, whose personal history shows her psychosexual development to be at a pregenital level. It is intended to demonstrate the type of clinical interpretations that enabled a change towards an oedipal transference in order to reach a psychoanalytic approach. Some of the psychoanalytic interpretations during the treatment process are described, related to several behavioural changes and a discrete structural change.

James Naiman

James Naiman

James Naiman
2156 Sherbrooke W. Apt. 6
Montreal H3H 1G7.

James Naiman  

SUMMARY

Evidence is presented that the Jocasta Sophocles depicted in Oedipus Rex is significantly different from the Jocasta of Freud's synopsis of the play. Sophocles' Jocasta prefers her son to her husband and this preference results in parricide and incest. The clinical implications of this difference are discussed in relation to a group of patients whose mothers resembled Sophocles' Jocasta. The analysis of one such patient is briefly presented. All these patients had difficulties at work and in their psychosexual development. It is argued that these difficulties resulted from the mother's attitude interfering with the dissolution of the Oedipus complex and that quite severe pathology does not necessarily mean pre-oedipal conflicts.

Anne Miller  

In these four volumes, Paul Ornstein presents a valuable compilation of the papers and letters of Heinz Kohut, representing all of his psychological writings with the exception of his three books. Included are 67 papers and 93 letters, as well as an index to the complete works of Heinz Kohut, prepared by Douglas W. Detrick.

In Ornstein's introduction, conceived of as a kind of 'guided tour', he traces the development of Kohut's work from the earliest papers, delineating important lines of thought and nodal points. Key papers are ascribed to each of the main lines of thought or are identified as nodal points. A chart illustrating the organization of the work appears in the third volume. (The introduction is presented in two parts, the first addressing Volumes I and II and the second, a continuation of the first, written for Volumes III and IV.)

In his exposition, Ornstein summarizes the principal ideas advocated in the main papers. He updates Kohut's earlier 'libido theory' language into later self-psychological terminology and, as well, identifies the beginnings of new theoretical and technical ideas.

[[RunningHead]]

Dream, Phantasy and Art

By Hanna Segal. With a foreword by Betty Joseph. New Library of Psychoanalysis. London & New York: Tavistock/Routledge, 1991. Pp. xiii & 120.

Richard Wollheim

Richard Wollheim

Richard Wollheim
Department of Philosophy, University of California Berkeley

Richard Wollheim  

On the face of it, Hanna Segal's new book consists of eight chapters, devoted to several different though clearly related topics, of which some, such as dreams, would in a classical perspective be regarded as of more central psychoanalytic interest than others, such as the artistic impulse, which would be relegated to applied psychoanalysis. In these chapters the different topics are taken to differing degrees of depth or elaboration, but in each case there is some supplementation or revision, amounting perhaps to no more than a telling change of emphasis in the author's known views or existing formulations. In all these respects, Dream, Phantasy and Art bears a striking resemblance to Freud's New Introductory Lectures.

Viewed in this light, Dream, Phantasy and Art has the value of the accumulated points that Segal makes, all of which are presented in that lean, taut style which she has made her own. Occasionally, it is true, the chapters show the signs of not having been written, or perhaps of not having been conceived, at a single stretch with the result that a particular point, instead of being made once decisively, is attempted several times and each time in a less than fully articulated fashion. But such departures from the normally forceful style of argumentation are rare, and at every turn in the narrative Segal has at her disposal case-material with precisely the right illustrative charge.

In Chapter I Segal takes up a suggestion already to be found in her earlier book, Delusion and Artistic Creativity, that the dream-work, which for Freud was the output of certain highly specific principles of mental functioning, might fruitfully be regarded as a special case of the far broader and presumably more improvisatory activity of working through. Here the point is made more explicit, and this allows Segal, in Chapter V, to make use of it in contrasting normal dreaming and the dream as this figures in the life of the psychotic. The psychotic is unable to work through his internal conflicts: his dream-work is correspondingly defective: and the dream as he dreams it, or in the use to which he puts it, stands on the very edge of hallucination.

Psychoanalytic Theories of Development

By Phyllis Tyson and Robert Tyson. New Haven: Yale University Press. 1990. Pp. 398.

Aaron Esman

Aaron Esman

Aaron H. Esman
Payne Whitney Clinic, NY Hospital–Cornell Medical Center
525 East 68th Street, New York, NY 10021

Aaron Esman  

From the day in 1897 when Freud lost faith in his 'neurotica' (i.e. his patients' accounts of their 'actual' sexual traumas) psychoanalysis became a science founded on a developmental theory of the mind. His discovery (or, if one wills, his codification) of infantile sexuality led in 1905 to the formulation of a distinctive and systematic conception of mental development as adumbrated in the Three Essays on the Theory of Sexuality —a conception that served, and for many continues to serve, as the fundamental underpinning of psychoanalytic ideas about the evolution of the mind and its conflicts. Though largely derived reconstructively from the analysis of adults, the Freudian theory of libidinal development, with a few elaborations by Abraham, Anna Freud and others, seemed to provide a schema that could readily be applied in child rearing and in clinical work with children. It served as the conceptual foundation for what came to be known as the 'genetic' point of view in psychoanalytic metapsychology.

The decades that had followed have, however, seen extensive additions to and revisions of Freud's model. These modifications have derived from two principal sources—analytic therapy and other modes of clinical work with children, and direct observation of infants and children in non-clinical settings. Both have yielded a wealth of data—and interpretations of data—that by the century's end have left us with a proliferation of viewpoints, some enriching Freud's schema, some in apparent conflict with it. Indeed, even within the broad mainstream of psychoanalysis as represented by the International Psychoanalytical Association, variant schools of analytic thought can be differentiated by their commitment to conceptions of child development that diverge sharply from the 'classical' Freudian model (e.g. Klein, Kohut).

Ilany Kogan

Ilany Kogan

Ilany Kogan
2 Mohaliver Street
Rehovot 76304
Israel

Ilany Kogan  

SUMMARY

This paper presents an explanation and illustration of the behavioural phenomenon of concretization through acting out. The phenomenon refers to the realization of fantasies and psychic conflicts pertaining to the traumatic past of Holocaust survivor parents, which might have been transmitted to the next generation narratively or beyond words. I have illustrated this phenomenon by material taken from the first stages of the analysis of a young man who shot and wounded his father during the latter's attempt to save him from suicide. The analytic experience facilitated the stabilization of ego boundaries and the emergence of a more secure sense of self.

[[RunningHead]]

Ambiguity as the Route to Shame

Silvia Sas

Silvia Sas

Silvia Amati Sas
40, Chemin des Coudriers
1209 Genève
Switzerland

Silvia Sas  

SUMMARY

In patients who have been exposed to extreme conditions, shame is connected with a dilemma of identity: it is indicative of the patient's conflict over his alienation, his adaptation to and familiarization with the conditions offered by an unacceptable frame.

Bleger's (1967) theoretical model, with the concepts of the symbiotic link, ambiguity and the ambiguous position (which precedes the classical Kleinian positions), dynamically combines the problem of the frame or context with that of the capacity of discrimination between the ego and its objects. Ambiguity is characterized by the adaptability, malleability, permeability and non-conflictuality which it confers on the personality. Shame appears as an alarm signal concerning ambiguity, connected with the ego's need to maintain its internal conflictuality, its capacity for integration and its sense of continuity.

Experiences of dependence and passivity, or ones which touch upon the dilemma between true and false, private and public, ethics and aesthetics, etc., may give rise to feelings of shame. In this paper it is suggested that a common denominator for the many different factors of shame may be found in the dynamics of ambiguity.

[[RunningHead]]

M. Masud R. Khan (1924–1989)

A. Limentani

A. Limentani

A. Limentani
38 Penshurst Gardens
Edgware
Middlesex HA8 9TP

A. Limentani  

Masud Khan, the enfant terrible of psychoanalysis of the second half of the century, died on 9 June 1989, leaving behind a remarkable degree of hostility and criticism mostly arising from the publication of his last book, When Spring Comes [ The Long Wait, USA, Ed.]. The more than justified sense of revulsion inspired by that book seems to have ensured an everlasting tendency in people, in and out of psychoanalytic circles, to discuss his life and behaviour, mixed with speculations as to what he was really like. As Janet Malcolm so aptly put it in a 1989 review in The New York Times: 'when one compares this outrageous and repellent book with the sane and civilized Privacy of the Self, one can only assume that something has happened to Mr Khan in the intervening fifteen years'.

This obituary is an attempt to satisfy some of that curiosity even if, for reasons of discretion, it will be somewhat limited. Indeed, I cannot meet Khan's desire which he expressed in a diary note dated 15 December 1981, which reads: 'In a strange way, I am leaving behind materials which I hope someone will put together and that will constitute the VERITY of Masud Khan'.

He was born in 1924 in Montgomery (now Sahival), Punjab, and grew up in what he called a 'primitive' estate and cattle farm. His father, Khan Bahadur Fazaldad Khan, also born in Punjab, in 1850, served in the British Army as a soldier, retiring as a major. Having had little education, he set about studying veterinary science to help him with cattle breeding. He later acquired some knowledge of law in order to become a justice of the peace. He had nine sons and five daughters from three marriages before marrying Masud's mother, a Persian dancer with a son from a previous marriage. A son was born in 1923, Masud in 1924, and a girl in 1926 who died when 16 years old.

This fourth marriage had been strongly opposed by the other children, and perhaps this was the reason for the family moving to a small village, Lyallpur, near Lahore, in 1937, where his father bought a large estate.

The Wish for a Sex Change

Agnès Oppenheimer

Agnès Oppenheimer

Agnès Oppenheimer
134, rue de Grenelle
75007 Paris

Agnès Oppenheimer  

Dear Sir,

I thank Dr Carder for his comments which give me the opportunity to clarify my positions and thoughts.

First, I would like to stress that I fully acknowledge Dr Stoller's contributions, even if I disagree on some points. He was not so much interested in transsexualism per se, but in the formation of core gender identity. Recognizing that most adult transsexuals do not fit the feminine boy syndrome he identified, he made a distinction between primary and secondary transsexualism.

Regarding primary feminine identity, Lothstein has observed boys with the same type of feminine behaviour dictated by their hatred of their male genitals. It is not because Stoller did not see any conflict that there is none: unconscious conflict is not a matter of observation. My adult patients say that they have no conflict, except with external reality, but the material demonstrates the contrary. How could a boy with an absent father, used by his mother for her own narcissistic purposes, not develop a narcissistic failure? He is not recognized for what he is. How would such a wound not create conflict? I do not think that anything but a false self can be constructed; a real gender self can only be built on body image and identifications.

I agree that my material is not fully psychoanalytical, but it shows that transsexuals are seeking narcissistic acknowledgment. Their acting seems to me to be a solution to wounds and conflicts. They overcome a deep trauma by drawing upon narcissistic sustenance from their transformation.

I have not been able, up until now, to convince transsexuals to undergo psychoanalysis; that may be part of their problem. They derive gratification that they are not ready to relinquish in favour of treatment.

This leads me to my last point. I do not see empathic attunement as contradictory to a framework of drive and defence. Empathy is not sharing the patient's convictions. Different parts of the self have to be heard. Even if I recognize the deficit dimension of their problem, deficit can meet conflict and defence—survival defence—for for the cohesion of the self.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Theory of Change

Leo Rangell

Leo Rangell

Leo Rangell
456 North Carmelina Avenue
Los Angeles
California 90049

Leo Rangell  

SUMMARY

The concept of change by psychoanalysis has undergone an historical process related to the course and development of psychoanalytic theory. Change has changed from an effect on symptoms, to underlying etiologic conflicts, to background character, to the functioning of psychic structural systems. The psychoanalytic process does not overlap with the process of change. The former may be present without the latter. The will to recover is accompanied by the unconscious need to cover. The methodology of change is examined with regard to process, agent(s), and intrapsychic results. Every analysis is a training and supervised analysis. With increasing autonomy of the ego, the patient, identifying with the analysing functioning of the analyst, becomes his own analyst. The theory of change is related to the psychoanalytic theory of neurosis of which it is part, and differs in alternative theories extant today. The dynamic occurrences I have described as bringing about change rest upon my view of 'total composite psychoanalytic theory' as evolved to the present.

SUMMARY

The case described here involves a patient who suffered a trauma while in the final stages of a successful analysis. The trauma temporarily eclipsed many of the gains of the analysis. Surprisingly, however, the regression caused by

453

this trauma led to further lifting of repression. This resulted in new information, greater affective expression and a greater therapeutic gain than would have otherwise been possible. The clinical technique devised for this special situation is described.

[[RunningHead]]

Reflections on Developments in the Theory of Psychoanalytic Technique

Joseph Sandler

Joseph Sandler

Joseph Sandler
35 Circus Road
London NW8

Joseph Sandler  

For the 1987 Congress of the IPA, held in Montreal, the then President—and now our Past President—Robert Wallerstein, chose the topic 'Analysis terminable and interminable: 50 years later', and gave as his Presidential Address a paper entitled 'One psychoanalysis or many?' As events have shown, both the topic of that Congress and the theme of Wallerstein's address were inspired choices, which set the stage for what was to be the next step in a process, i.e. his choice of topic for the 1989 Rome Congress of 'Common ground in psychoanalysis: clinical aims and process'. It was therefore natural to select, for the present Congress, the topic of 'Psychic change: developments in the theory of psychoanalytic technique'. During this Congress we have been treated to a wealth of presentations and discussions, and we will have much to take away and digest. The success of our meetings makes it a particular privilege to give the opening presentation in this plenary session.

In his introduction to the prepublished papers, Harold Blum (1991) pointed out that 'All the goals of clinical analysis converge in psychic change whether conceptualized in restoring the ego to optimal function, structural change, or personality reorganization …' He posed the questions of how, why and when change occurs, and what the technical principles and specific technical agents of change are, and went on to comment:

Further considerations lead to the theory of technique. What are the underlying assumptions and models that guide the analyst's listening, observing, and organizing the analytic data? No analyst can approach the analytic situation without some theoretical notions and convictions, while, at the same time, every analysis is a potentially new learning experience for the analyst as well as the patient.

What, we may ask, do we really mean by a theory of technique? Should we say that such a theory is the same for all analysts, a set of technical principles and rules of procedure laid down by the IPA? I doubt whether this is an altogether satisfactory response. Yet there is no doubt that the IPA can, at least in relation to the training and education of candidates in its Component Societies and Institutes, lay down certain minimal criteria for the conduct of the candidate's analysis in order to ensure that he or she has the possibility of obtaining an appropriate and adequate analytic experience, so essential for work as an analyst.

[[RunningHead]]

The Refinding of Theory in Clinical Practice

Michael Parsons

Michael Parsons

Michael Parsons
2 Quadrant Grove
London NW5 4JN

Michael Parsons  

SUMMARY

The analyst's use of his preconscious is central to understanding how clinical practice in psychoanalysis is informed by theory. One view is that we have a definitive conception of our theory, from which we derive our clinical practice by largely preconscious deliberation. Another view sees psychoanalytic concepts as elastic, related to each other along dimensions of meaning in a finite but flexible theoretical space. We do not, on this view, determine once for all what our theory is, but continually have to discover it by exploring our preconscious as we watch ourselves at work.

Some detailed clinical material shows how I observed the evolution of my use of theory in a particular case. I had to refind theory which I already knew; familiar concepts had to become suprising. This also allowed theoretical understanding to emerge which was new to me.

This implies an act of personal engagement with our theoretical concepts, rather than the pseudo-objective application of what are taken for facts. A survey of various positions in the philosophy of science shows that this is entirely consistent with the nature of scientific activity.

The Analyst's Transference Imagery

Pierre Doucet

Pierre Doucet

Pierre Doucet
5 Avenue Peronne
Outremont
Montreal
Quebec H3S 1X7

Pierre Doucet  

SUMMARY

The countertransference is a concept whose meaning has changed considerably. Freud and his followers regarded it as a negative reaction in the treatment which the analyst had to master. Since 1950 and Paula Heimann's pioneering paper, the countertransference has been understood and used more positively and has come to be seen as an essential tool for achieving completeness in the work of analysis. In line with this approach, this paper describes a countertransference reaction which has proved to be useful. It concerns the appearance of images of houses in the analyst. These houses correspond to important experiences revived by the analysand's material. The emotional content of these images links up with an identical content in the patient. An interpretation based on this experience has an enhanced therapeutic strength and effect. The paper concludes with a case study and a theoretical discussion of the phenomenon.

[[RunningHead]]

Enclaves and Excursions

Edna O'Shaughnessy

Edna O'Shaughnessy

Edna O'Shaughnessy
22 Heath Hurst Road
London NW3 2RX

Edna O'Shaughnessy  

SUMMARY

This paper describes and names as 'enclaves' and 'excursions' two deteriorations of the psychoanalytic situation, which occur when an analyst exceeds the limited, partial acting out inevitable in clinical work. Under pressure from his patient an analyst may unwittingly turn the analysis into a refuge from disturbance, i.e. an enclave. If he succumbs to pressures to move away from, instead of towards, knowing what is psychically urgent, an analyst may turn the analysis into a series of flights, i.e. an excursion. Unrecognized, these deformations interfere with or even halt the therapeutic process, which requires that the analyst find a way of working which accommodates both the patient's communication of, and need for, a refuge or flight with the analyst, while at the same time analysing it.

SUMMARY

This paper highlights features of the work on autism of Tustin and others pertaining to the analysis of adult patients. Several clinical illustrations from the analysis of neurotic, borderline and psychotic patients emphasizing the survival function of autistic shapes, objects and delusions are presented. The need for further discrimination between autistic states of mind and other primitive mental states is recommended.

Dorothy Holmes  

SUMMARY

Given the enduring primacy of race in western cultures, it is always a factor in any psychotherapeutic situation. In this paper, the role of race in elucidating transference manifestations is explored. Vignettes and clinical narratives are used to illustrate the point that race can be a useful vehicle for the expression and elaboration of transferences of defence, of drive derivative and of object ties. Several factors are discussed which influence whether and to what degree race serves as a point of engagement for transference reactions.

[[RunningHead]]

Countertransference: The Analyst's Retreat from the Patient's Vantage Point

Evelyne Schwaber

Evelyne Schwaber

Evelyne Albrecht Schwaber
4 Welland Road
Brookline
Mass. 02146

Evelyne Schwaber  

SUMMARY

As analysts, we may agree with certain basic tenets: we should not impose our truths, whether or not theory-laden; we should maintain the focus on the patient's inner reality, not on how he or she lives in the world—out there; we should be prepared to be surprised, to learn something that hadn't occurred to us; we should look for multiple cues in the clinical data, pay

359

attention to affect and state as may be expressed within the moment, and listen for subtleties that may convey something about how we are being experienced in the transference.

But again and again, we fail to adhere to these precepts. Despite our best intentions, we seem to have a fundamental disinclination to maintaining these positions. I have tried to consider some reasons for this difficulty and its relation to the countertransference. Drawing upon several clinical examples, I have attempted to examine ways of enhancing our attunement to verbal and non-verbal cues from the patient which may direct us, sooner, to dimensions of our countertransference responses otherwise overlooked.

Psychic Change and its Clinical Evaluation

Jorge García

Jorge García

Jorge E. García Badaracco
Libertad 1370 13 'B'
1016 Buenos Aires
Argentina

Jorge García  

SUMMARY

The author looks at the question of psychic change from the viewpoint of difficulties involved in this change, starting with a short historical overview. He discusses 'the difficult patient as a specialist in resisting change' and describes the clinical characteristics of such patients over and above their psychopathological diagnoses. Some specific clinical cases are described to clarify the theoretical ideas.

The author then covers the nature of changes best studied within the field of transference and countertransference. Finally, he examines change as a process of change and suggests that qualitative differences exist between psychic functions relating to 'traumas' in narcissistic constraints. These differences are apparent in psychic content. The connexions between interpretation, insight, anxiety and change are described. The nature of the negative therapeutic reaction is discussed. The notion of an underlying deficiency in the development of genuine egoistic resources required to bring about psychic change is examined.

Concepts of Termination and Post-Termination Patient-Analyst Contact

Joseph Schachter

Joseph Schachter

Joseph Schachter
401 Shady Avenue
Pittsburgh
PA 15206

Joseph Schachter  

SUMMARY

This paper examines the relationship of concepts of termination to the role of post-termination patient-analyst contact. The concept of termination seems to have been derived historically from at least three elements: the conception of the analytic process; the goal of analysis; and the therapeutic outcome of analysis. Evidence from clinical experience fails to substantiate analysts' belief that post-termination patient-analyst contacts are likely to be damaging to the patient. Most analysts structure termination so that subsequent contact with the patient is conditional upon the patient's need for additional analytic help. However, they themselves seek multiple post-termination contacts with their own training analyst. Periodic post-termination patient-analyst contacts as extensions of the analytic situation provide the analyst with an improved perspective for evaluating the prior analytic work and provide the patient with evidence of the analyst's continuing physicianly concern for his/her well-being. The latter extends the therapeutic alliance which had supported the earlier analytic work and which probably enhances the maintenance of analytic gains and facilitates continued self-analytic work.

Dear Mr. Tuckett,

This 'letter to the editor' is a consequence of my having had the privilege of reading Dr Evelyne Schwaber's paper: 'Countertransference: The Analyst's Retreat from the Patient's Vantage Point' (Int. J. Psycho-Anal., 73: 349–362).

One of the memorable moments in my acquaintance with Dr Schwaber was the recall of a day, more years ago than either of us would like to count, when Dr Schwaber was a resident in psychiatry. She and I were on one of the pediatric wards at the Boston City Hospital, observing what was then known as a 'failure to thrive' infant, an infant of some 7–8 months of age that had gained only a bare few pounds since birth. In addition to its malnourishment and frailty, the infant was difficult to feed, sleeping very poorly, although crying little. Our interest, however, was drawn to the eyes of the infant. The lids never closed, the aperture was narrowed but the eyes of the infant were ever-vigilant—watching—always watching us, as we were watching him.

The Dialectically Constituted/decentred Subject of Psychoanalysis. II. the Contributions of Klein and Winnicott

Thomas Ogden

Thomas Ogden

Thomas H. Ogden
1721 Scott Street
San Francisco
CA 94115

Thomas Ogden  

SUMMARY

The present paper focuses on the contributions of Klein and Winnicott to a psychoanalytic conception of the dialectically constituted and decentred subject. Klein's idea of 'positions' represents a conception of the subject constituted in the creative and negating dialectical interplay of fundamentally different modes of attributing meaning to experience. The Kleinian subject is decentred in psychological space and in psychoanalytic time. The concept of projective identification provides the elements of a theory of the creation of the subject in the context of a psychological-interpersonal dialectic.

The subject for Winnicott is not coincident with the individual psyche: 'There is no such thing as an infant [apart from the mother]'. Winnicott's notion of the creation of the subject in the psychological space between mother and infant involves a conception of the on-going constitution of the subject in the simultaneity of forms of dialectical tension between at-one-ment

624

and separateness, internality and externality, I and me, I and Thou.
